answers.intake_q2: What symptoms have you been experiencing since the onset?
answers.intake_a2: cough with phelgm, sore throat, runny nose, fever, chills
answers.intake_q3: Have you noticed any shortness of breath or chest pain?
answers.intake_q4: Have you had any recent exposure to anyone with confirmed pneumonia or COVID-19?
answers.intake_q5: Have you taken any medications or treatments for your symptoms so far?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any worsening difficulty breathing, confusion, or persistent high fever?
answers.intake_q7: Do you have any chronic lung conditions like asthma or COPD, or other significant medical problems?
answers.intake_q8: Have you had any recent COVID-19 testing or chest imaging done?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any blood in your phlegm or a sudden worsening of your cough?
answers.intake_a9: the cough is bad, but no blood in my phlegm
answers.intake_summary: A 5-day history of cough with phlegm, sore throat, runny nose, fever, and chills following exposure to pneumonia or COVID-19. Mild shortness of breath without severe symptoms or chronic lung disease. No prior testing done. Symptoms consistent with community-acquired pneumonia.

gating_notes: renal disease status is yes/unknown (dose adjustment / avoid certain meds)
liver disease status is yes/unknown (avoid hepatotoxic meds / adjust dosing)

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 5-day history of cough productive of phlegm, sore throat, runny nose, fever, and chills. She reports mild shortness of breath but denies chest pain, hemoptysis, confusion, or persistent high fever. She has had recent exposure to someone with confirmed pneumonia or COVID-19. She has taken DayQuil for symptom relief. She has no chronic lung conditions but does have underlying renal disease and a penicillin allergy. No recent COVID-19 testing or chest imaging has been performed. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
Working diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia, likely bacterial given the productive cough, fever, chills, and exposure history. Mild shortness of breath is present but no severe respiratory distress or systemic signs.

Differential: 
1. Community-acquired bacterial pneumonia 
2. Viral upper respiratory infection with secondary bacterial infection 
3. Acute bronchitis 
4. COVID-19 infection 

P: 
 - Medication support:
   - Plan assessment:
     - Working impression based on telephone history. Management below is limited by telephone-only evaluation (no vitals/labs available during the call).
   - Rx candidates:
     - Supportive care — symptom relief and supportive management
   - Gating flags:
     - Pregnancy: no
     - Renal disease: unknown
     - Liver disease: unknown
   - Return precautions:
     - Go to urgent care/ED now for severe or rapidly worsening symptoms, trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, fainting, or inability to keep fluids down.
     - Seek urgent in-person care for new high fever, severe pain, dehydration concerns, or any red-flag symptoms discussed during the call.
   - Testing:
     - If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).
- First-line medication: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days, avoiding beta-lactams due to penicillin allergy and considering renal disease (dosing adjustment may be needed based on renal function). 
- Alternatives: 
 • Levofloxacin 500 mg orally once daily for 5 days, with caution in renal disease 
 • Azithromycin 500 mg orally on day 1, then 250 mg daily on days 2–5, if tolerated and no macrolide resistance concerns 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid penicillin and related beta-lactam antibiotics due to allergy. Renal disease requires careful dosing and monitoring of doxycycline or fluoroquinolones. No pregnancy concerns. 
- Return precautions: Seek urgent care if worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, persistent high fever, or inability to tolerate oral medications. 
- Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

Conditions A to Z
Pneumonia 
Pneumonia is inflammation of the lungs, usually caused by an infection. Most people get better in 2 to 4 weeks, but babies, older people, and people with heart or lung conditions are at risk of getting seriously ill and may need treatment in hospital. 
Symptoms of pneumonia 
Symptoms of pneumonia can start suddenly or gradually over a few days.
They include:
a cough – you may cough up yellow or green mucus (phlegm) shortness of breath a high temperature chest pain an aching body feeling very tired loss of appetite making wheezing noises when you breathe – babies may also make grunting noises feeling confused – this is common in older people 
Ur

evidence.title: Pneumonia | Pneumonia Symptoms | Signs of Pneumonia | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
e , liver disease , and kidney disease . 
What are the symptoms of pneumonia? 
The symptoms of pneumonia can range from mild to severe and include:
Fever 
Chills 
Cough, usually with phlegm (a type of thick mucus made in your lungs) 
Shortness of breath 
Chest pain when you breathe or cough 
Nausea and/or vomiting 
Diarrhea 
The symptoms can vary for different groups. Newborns and infants may not show any signs of the infection. Others may vomit and have a fever and cough. They might seem sick, with no energy, or be restless.
Older adults and people who have serious illnesses or weak immune systems may have fewer and milder symptoms. They may even have a lower-than-normal temperature. Older adults who have pneumonia sometimes may feel weak or suddenly get confused.
What other problems can pneumonia cause? 
Sometimes pneumonia can cause serious complications such as:
Bacteremia, which hap

evidence.title: Video: Sick? Take Precautions | Respiratory Illnesses | CDC
evidence.snippet:
lp stop the spread if you get sick with a respir... 
Aug. 26, 2025 
Sample messages 
For older adults and their families 
Message 1 
Respiratory illnesses can pose serious health risks to people who are 65 or older because immune systems weaken with age, making it harder to fight off infections. If you're 65 or older, it's important to seek medical care if you get sick. Learn why timely treatment is crucial to your health at https://bit.ly/4lWF8qA 
Message 2 
The older we get, the weaker our immune system can become — making it harder to fight off infections. So if you’re 65 or older, getting quick medical attention when you have

evidence.title: Chest infection - NHS
evidence.snippet:
65 or have a serious long-term health condition):
ask a GP about the annual flu vaccine ask if you should have the pneumococcal vaccine – this helps prevent pneumonia try to quit smoking if you smoke cut down on how much alcohol you drink 
Page last reviewed: 21 June 2023
Next review due: 21 June 2026
